definitions.technical: A consensus property ensuring that a distributed network reaches agreement on state transitions even when up to one-third of participating nodes are faulty or adversarial. BFT systems achieve safety (no conflicting states are finalized) and liveness (the system continues producing new states) under this threshold. The one-third bound is a theoretical limit proved by Lamport, Shostak, and Pease (1982): no deterministic protocol can tolerate more than f Byzantine faults among 3f+1 nodes.
definitions.lay: A system designed to keep working correctly even when some participants are dishonest or go offline. As long as fewer than one-third of the participants are bad actors, the system reaches agreement and keeps running. Named after a thought experiment about Byzantine generals who must coordinate an attack despite traitors in their ranks.
definitions.parenthetical: (a property ensuring network agreement despite faulty or malicious nodes, up to a one-third threshold)
